1. Introduction
---------------

Artificial Intelligence (AI) has been experiencing unprecedented growth in numerous fields, including healthcare, [fintech](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/fintech "Learn more about fintech from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), government, logistics, information technology, education, defence, manufacturing, automobile industry ([Allen et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib3), [Hermann, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib37), [Jobin et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib47), [Morley et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib68)). AI influences [industries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in institutional, political, and cultural contexts, which impacts individual and social/economic transformations ([Davenport & Kirby, 2016;](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib21)[Krishnan, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib51); [Hunkenschroer & Luetge, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib44)). As a result, AI design, development, and deployment pose many challenges to the individual- well-being and [autonomy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/autonomy "Learn more about autonomy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), labour productivity, societal/economic- impacts on safety, reliability, inclusion, and unbiased decision-making processes ([Bertino et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib8), [Damioli et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib18), [Goodman and Flaxman, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib34)). AI now functions in partnership with humans in day-to-day activities. Therefore, AI needs to consider [human relationships](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/human-relation "Learn more about human relationships from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), emotions and consequential decision-making processes ([Webster and Ivanov, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib92), [Urquhart et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib89), [Malkin et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib56)). Various malicious prospects exist for AI systems to be biased, discriminant, and vulnerable to data breaches ([Floridi et al., 2019;](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib30)[Jobin et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib48)). It results in compromised privacy and security, leading to reputation and confidentiality risks ([McGrath and Gupta, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib58), [Rogerson et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib79), [McLaren, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib59)).

AI deployment concerns have ignited various government institutions, research agencies, analysts' groups and technology giants to formulate and draft AI ethics-led [sustainability frameworks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/sustainability-framework "Learn more about sustainability frameworks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") suitable to their conditions and ecosystems ([Hoffman and Masucci, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib40), [Jerome, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib46), [Hleg, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib39), [Yeung, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib98)). These institutions have published declarative standards and statements for ethical AI deployments in public. This work emphasises the urgent need to comprehend and study available AI ethics-led [sustainability frameworks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/sustainability-framework "Learn more about sustainability frameworks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to design, develop, and deploy ethical AI systems. We are interested in exploring the cohesions of AI ethics-led [sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") frameworks documented/framed by many institutions and agencies. This article highlights the current philosophical and practical ethical interests of AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks when developing effective unities for AI deployments. The principal objective of the paper is to investigate the following research questions:
**RQ1**

What ethical aspects are explored in the existing literature in conceptualising and designing AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks?

**RQ2**

What are the central themes of AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks in declarative standards and statements published by various institutions?

**RQ3**

What practical and mature AI ethics-led sustainability toolkits are available from different technology and research organisations to translate the general AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks/guidelines into simple AI ethics design, architecture, and code while developing AI systems?

The paper aims to begin by answering the formulated research questions and highlighting how AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks influence current AI deployments. The paper primarily underlines AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks' most common and cohesive elements: explainability, fairness, governance, privacy, and inclusiveness. The paper leverages a PRISMA-based [systematic literature review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic literature review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the AI ethics body of knowledge using 30 relevant shortlisted research papers.

The paper is organised as follows. [Section 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#sec0005) introduces AI ethics and AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks as subjects and presents the foundation of the paper. It also represents the definitions and foundations of the AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks. Existing AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks (from federal/government agencies, standards societies/groups, technology giants, and analysts' groups) have also been extensively surveyed to identify common principles. [Section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#sec0010) of the article highlights the PRISMA-based AI ethics-led sustainability framework's literature review covering the current state of the literature, article selection methods and database metrics. It aims to analyse AI ethics-led sustainability framework principles using software based on thematic code analysis. [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#sec0020) covers the consolidated view of commonly applied methods, theories, and the industry context with objective(s) and previously published work findings. It discusses the unique market analysis of the practical AI ethics toolkits to translate the AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks to reality and become a handy tool for managers. Finally, [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#sec0045) presents the conclusive research and managerial implications for designing, developing, and deploying successful AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks and toolkits.

2. AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks
------------------------------------------

Today's society and ecosystems are becoming the testimony of AI-led transformations to execute many tasks that often require [human intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/human-intelligence "Learn more about human intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). AI, which touches human lives, raises questions about ethical principles of [autonomy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/autonomy "Learn more about autonomy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), biasedness, fairness, and accountability, issues found in various sector-based literature ([Jobin et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib47)). Ethics as a concept is wide-ranging and spans utilitarian, deontological, and virtue-based theories. [Utilitarianism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/utilitarianism "Learn more about Utilitarianism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") refers to consequentialism-focused ethical choice, which defines the actions of right and wrong deeds based on outcomes. Deontological ethics is the study of 'deon' (Greek Word meaning duties), where the alternatives are chosen based on moral values of accountability, commitment, and rules rather than the consequential action ([Ferrell et al., 1989](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib29), [May and Pauli, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib57)). Virtue ethics underscore the importance of the character or act of a virtuous individual facing ethical circumstances ([Burmeister, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib12); [Yang & Lee, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib97)). AI ethics specifies the utilitarian, deontological, and virtue-based guidelines necessary to manage the moral responsibilities and duties of AI developers, promoters, and architects. Established theories are used to develop AI ethics-led [sustainability frameworks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/sustainability-framework "Learn more about sustainability frameworks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and principles to code the actions and responsibilities of human behaviour performed by machines that lead to artificial moral machines (AMAs). When machines perform actions, it is not easy to address the issues and ethical binaries of right/wrong or good/bad ([Siau & Wang, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib83)).

AI and its applications in the research and managerial domain are nothing new; they have existed for many decades since the 1950s. Similarly, researchers and practitioners from the same timeframes contested the lack of associated risks of defined ethical consequences in the AI domain ([Floridi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib31); [Yang et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib96)). The latest topics of ethical interest entail: i) algorithmic biasedness (acts of AI systems that promote racial, social, and gender discrimination), ii) explainability (non-transparent, unexplainable algorithmic outcomes by the AI system), iii) autonomy (violations of rights and freedoms, struggle to identify the responsible entity), iv) privacy (individual data invasion driving AI [data lakes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/data-lake "Learn more about data lakes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and workflows), v) [beneficence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/beneficence "Learn more about beneficence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (machine obligation to protect a user's moral rules and rights), vi) non-maleficence (obligation to not harming the AI system users), and vi) [justice](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/justice "Learn more about justice from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Floridi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib31), [Siau and Wang, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib83)). Various AI ethics guidance manuscripts are available from bodies and agencies like analyst groups, technology companies, federal agencies, and standard/research organisations. These documents cover algorithmic biasedness, explainability, autonomy, privacy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice that lay the foundations of principles and frameworks ([Whittlestone et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib93)). Integrating the practical and theoretical aspects of the AI ethics principles and frameworks is broad and not specific to consequential scenarios. The principles vary in demographics, countries, culture, and societal settings ([Carrillo, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib13), [Morley et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib67)). Despite having many principles and framework documents available, there is a dearth of toolkits to implement the guidelines. This makes the AI ethics document very abstract and out of reach for real AI developers and architects to make tangible design decisions ([Floridi et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib30)). The researchers envisage a balanced approach to understanding ethics, its principles, interaction, and outcomes with AI machines between humans and machines. AI ethics toolkits/simulators must utilise the [early warning system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/early-warning-system "Learn more about early warning system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to forecast the anticipated dangers and ensure open engagement and a transparent consensus-based approach ([Schif et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib82)).

### 2.1. AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks and common principles

As AI is adopted and diffused in numerous areas, with some areas presenting a risk, the burden [rests on](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/methapyrilene "Learn more about rests on from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") researchers and practitioners to lay down principles and frameworks of AI ethics. The central theme of this section is to provide a glimpse of available well-accepted AI ethics-led [sustainability frameworks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/sustainability-framework "Learn more about sustainability frameworks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with their associated principles, goals, and objectives. Based on the literature survey, authors consolidated and grouped all surveyed 28 AI ethics-led [sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") frameworks under 4 categories ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#fig0005)) coming from institutions like i) standard societies/groups, ii) technology organisations, iii) analyst groups, and iv) federal/government agencies (by country).

![Image 11: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2950370124000038-gr1.jpg)

2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2950370124000038-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Surveyed AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks.

Researchers, practitioners, the analyst community, activists, and lawmakers are putting forward their best efforts to produce manuals, process documents, write regulations, and create standards in AI ethics-led sustainability frameworks. Technical/standards institutions like the High-Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence (HLEG), the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), the World Commission on the Ethics of Scientific Knowledge and Technology (COMEST), and [AINOW (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib2) contributed significantly to the AI ethics led sustainability frameworks. All the reports from these institutions possess a common thread of human autonomy, fairness and explicability, safety, accountability, power and control, beneficence, etc., as core principles ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#fig0010)). Technology giants like Intel, Facebook (Meta), [Accenture (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib1), IBM, Microsoft, and [Google (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#bib35) are not far behind in the race and released their respective declarations to adhere to the common principles of explainability, fairness, inclusion, accountability, governance, data protection, and privacy design ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#fig0015)). There is a need for standard AI guideline documents emphasising humanity, society, environmental well-being, values, safety, reliability, [inclusivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/inclusivity "Learn more about inclusivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), non-discrimination and abidance of [laws and regulations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/laws-and-regulation "Learn more about laws and regulations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") across the regions. Analyst groups like KPMG, Delloite, Forrester, [BCG](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/mycobacterium-bovis-bcg "Learn more about BCG from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), Gartner and PWC reciprocated the vitality of integrity, explainability, fairness, resilience, bias prevention, interpretability, explainability, robustness, and security in the same voice across the surveyed reports ([Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#fig0020)). Countries like Singapore, China, Australia, Finland, [Japan](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/japan "Learn more about Japan from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), Canada, the [USA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/united-states-of-america "Learn more about USA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the UK, and [India](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/india "Learn more about India from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can access the ethical framework through manuals and vision documents ([Fig. 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2950370124000038#fig0025)).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Policymakers play a crucial role in shaping regulations and standards that govern AI ethics, ensuring alignment with societal values and addressing potential risks.
Collaboration with tech developers is vital to create ethical AI systems. Their insights drive innovation while adhering to ethical standards, fostering responsible development practices.
Engaging with patient advocacy groups ensures that AI technologies meet patient needs, enhance care quality, and address ethical concerns related to data privacy and treatment equity.
A multidisciplinary framework integrates diverse expertise from technology, ethics, law, and healthcare, facilitating comprehensive solutions that address the multifaceted challenges posed by AI.
